skill.name: gitlab-automation
skill.description: Automate GitLab project management, issues, merge requests, CI/CD pipelines, branches, and user lookup through Rube MCP tools.
skill.summary: Use Rube MCP to coordinate common GitLab project, issue, merge request, pipeline, branch, and user workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many Ruby backtick and weak-crypto indicators, but the reviewed file is markdown documentation and the backticks are inline tool names, parameters, and examples. No prompt injection attempt or malicious exfiltration intent was found. The real risk is operational: the skill guides authenticated GitLab actions through Rube MCP, including issue creation, project creation, branch creation, and user lookup, so it should publish with a clear external-service and write-action warning.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated GitLab Write Operations Through External MCP
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to connect Rube MCP to GitLab and use tools that can create projects, create branches, create issues, and update issues. This is legitimate for a GitLab automation skill, but mistakes or overbroad prompts could modify live GitLab data using the user authorized account.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly require Rube MCP GitLab authorization and list write-capable GitLab tools. The behavior is expected for the skill, but it still creates live operational risk.

medium_findings.title: External Service Dependency for GitLab Access
medium_findings.description: The setup directs users to add the Rube MCP endpoint and complete GitLab OAuth through that service. This introduces a third-party service dependency for authenticated GitLab operations and should be disclosed to users.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 20 directly names the external MCP URL and the surrounding setup describes GitLab OAuth. The evidence supports a disclosure concern, not a finding of malicious exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Command Execution Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many Ruby shell backtick findings. Review found markdown inline code for Rube tool names, GitLab parameters, and examples rather than executable Ruby or shell code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is SKILL.md documentation, and the flagged backticks surround tool slugs and parameter names. No executable Ruby source or shell command invocation is present in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Identifier Text False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged weak cryptographic algorithm terms on several lines, but review found frontmatter, GitLab field names, search parameters, member listing text, and issue creation documentation. No cryptographic implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are frontmatter or GitLab parameter descriptions, not code that selects cryptographic algorithms. The skill contains no crypto routines.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Findings Are GitLab Metadata Queries
low_findings.description: The analyzer reported reconnaissance on lines that describe branch verification and CI pipeline filters. These are normal GitLab workflow queries and do not inspect the local host system.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines describe validating GitLab branch references and filtering invalid pipeline configurations. They do not collect local machine, OS, or environment data.

dangerous_patterns.title: Live GitLab Mutation Tools Documented
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents write-capable GitLab tools for creating projects, creating branches, creating issues, and updating issues. These operations can affect production repositories if used without confirmation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The tool names and workflow descriptions clearly identify state-changing GitLab operations. The pattern is dangerous only when prompts or permissions are too broad.

content.value_statement: GitLab work often spans issues, merge requests, pipelines, branches, and user records. This skill provides structured Rube MCP workflows for common GitLab operations.
actual_capabilities: Guides setup of a Rube MCP GitLab connection through the GitLab toolkit.
Lists, creates, and updates GitLab issues with labels, assignees, state changes, and pagination guidance.
Lists and filters merge requests by project, state, branch, author, reviewer, and labels.
Helps find projects, inspect repositories, create branches, and review commit history.
Monitors GitLab pipelines and jobs with status, ref, date, and scope filters.
Looks up GitLab users, user status, project members, and project users.
limitations: Requires an active Rube MCP server and an authorized GitLab connection.
Available actions depend on the user GitLab permissions and project plan.
The skill is a workflow guide and does not include executable source code.
Tool schemas may change, so the skill requires searching current Rube tool schemas first.

use_cases.title: Triage Project Issues
use_cases.description: Find open issues, adjust labels, assign owners, and close resolved work across a GitLab project.

use_cases.title: Review Merge Requests
use_cases.description: Filter merge requests by branch, state, reviewer, or label before planning code review work.

use_cases.title: Monitor Delivery Health
use_cases.description: Check recent pipelines and jobs for a project, then narrow results by status, ref, or date.

prompt_templates.title: List My Projects
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the GitLab automation skill to find my accessible GitLab projects. Search the current Rube tool schemas first, then list matching projects for this query: [project name].

prompt_templates.title: Create an Issue
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GitLab issue in [project]. Use title [title], description [description], and labels [labels]. Verify the project ID before creating it.

prompt_templates.title: Review Merge Requests
prompt_templates.prompt: Find open merge requests in [project] for [target branch]. Include author, reviewer, labels, and draft status. Use filters to keep the result concise.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Pipeline Failures
prompt_templates.prompt: Investigate failed pipelines for [project] on [branch] since [date]. List failed jobs, pipeline IDs, commit refs, and likely next actions.

output_examples.input: List open issues labeled bug in the mobile-app project.
output_examples.output: The assistant returns a filtered issue list with issue numbers, titles, assignees, labels, and current state.

output_examples.input: Check failed pipelines on the main branch from this week.
output_examples.output: The assistant summarizes matching pipeline runs, failed jobs, commit references, and suggested follow-up checks.

output_examples.input: Find active project members whose name matches Maya.
output_examples.output: The assistant returns matching members with GitLab user identifiers, usernames, access context, and status where available.

best_practices: Search current Rube tool schemas before each workflow because tool parameters can change.
Verify project IDs, issue IIDs, branch names, and user IDs before write operations.
Use filters and pagination to avoid incomplete or overly broad GitLab results.
anti_patterns: Do not run broad write operations without confirming the target project and identifiers.
Do not assume labels are appended when using fields that replace all existing labels.
Do not request admin-only or paid-plan fields without checking permissions and plan support.

faq.question: Does this skill require GitLab credentials?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires an active GitLab connection through Rube MCP using the GitLab toolkit.

faq.question: Can it create or update GitLab issues?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents workflows for creating issues, updating state, changing labels, and assigning users.

faq.question: Can it approve or merge merge requests?
faq.answer: No evidence found in the skill that it performs approval or merge actions.

faq.question: Can it inspect CI/CD pipelines?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports listing pipelines, retrieving a single pipeline, and listing pipeline jobs.

faq.question: Why does it say to search tools first?
faq.answer: Rube MCP tool schemas can change. Searching tools first helps the assistant use current parameters.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a warning because it can guide authenticated GitLab changes through an external MCP service.
